project_name: Implications of Polyamine and Glucosylceramide Transport for Parkinson's Disease (IMPACT-PD)
project_description: Our research program investigates the molecular mechanisms of Parkinson's disease (PD) by dissecting how PD-associated genes—specifically ATP13A2, ATP10B, and GBA1—regulate lysosomal homeostasis. As part of the ASAP Collaborative Research Network (CRN), our team employs a multi-disciplinary approach—integrating human iPSC-derived midbrain models, rodent systems, and purified protein complexes—to determine how lysosomal dysfunction triggers cellular states associated with neurodegeneration. A primary focus of this project is characterizing the transition of astrocytes toward a reactive, pro-inflammatory state and the subsequent impact on dopaminergic neuron survival. By generating large-scale transcriptomic (RNA-seq), chromatin accessibility (ATAC-seq), and methylome (bisulfite sequencing) datasets, we aim to map the pathways linking genetic loss-of-function (e.g., ATP13A2 c.1306) to chronic neuroinflammation and impaired lysosomal proteolysis. Ultimately, this work seeks to identify central regulators of these pathogenic states to provide new therapeutic strategies for PD.
dataset_title: DNA methylome profiling (Bisulfite Sequencing) of human iPSC-derived midbrain astrocytes in ATP13A2 c.1306deficiency models
dataset_description: This dataset provides base-resolution DNA methylation profiles generated via Whole-Genome Bisulfite Sequencing (WGBS) of human iPSC-derived midbrain astrocytes. The study contrasts the methylome of ATP13A2 c.1306 mutants with Wild Type (WT) controls. The objective was to evaluate overall DNA methylation levels and regional epigenetic modifications associated with the genotype. This dataset is designed to be integrated with companion RNA-seq and ATAC-seq data to provide a multi-omic characterization of the model.
